coredata.dc:description: As same classification of tin-based chalcogenides system, all of the SnX (X=S/Se/Te) should possess similar structural characteristics by our conventional view. However, the experimental SnS&SnSe possess orthorhombic-phase structure while SnTe refers to other cubic-phase. How to explain above structural-transition and quantify the impact on their electrochemical behaviors are remained challenge. Herein, such phenomena are investigated by both experimental-tools and theoretical-calculations. The results show that the integration of S/Se into Sn would trigger serious lattice-distortion, bonding-broken and thus phase-transition from cubic to orthorhombic SnS&SnSe due to their large difference on atomic-radius and electronegativity. While fusing Te atom into Sn to form the SnTe enables a well maintained cubic-structure phase like Sn matrix owing to their similar physicochemical properties. Benefitted from above structure characteristics, the SnTe enables to keep Sn-like metallic conductivity (3.31×103 S m−1), larger tap-density (6.48 g cm−3) and better reaction-kinetic, thus delivering better cyclability (698 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles) and rate performance (236 mAh g−1 at 10A g−1) for LIBs. Besides, the SnTe even exhibits higher pseudo-capacitance contribution (94%) and thereby, the assembled AC//SnTe Li-ion capacitor enables high energy & power density (114 Wh kg−1, 7000 W kg−1) and superior cyclability (92.7%-retention after 8000 cycles) for lithium-storage. The above understanding of structure-transition and electrochemical-evolution may open avenue for material design towards advanced energy storage.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of orthorhombic SnS, SnSe, and cubic SnTe. Comparison of the XRD Rietveld refinements (a-c), phase structure information (d), Raman spectra (e), SAED and HRTEM images (f-h) between SnX products.
Comparison of the atomic radius, electronegativity, energy formation and structural evolution (a), calculated phonon dispersion relations (b) between SnS, SnSe and SnTe.

Electronic structure evolution and bonding interaction characterization. Comparison of the plots of electron location function (a), high resolution XPS spectra (b), calculated density of state (c), powder conductivity measurement (d), TG analysis (e), and compaction density (f) between SnS, SnSe and SnTe products.

Electrochemical behavior characterization. Comparison of the discharge/charge profiles (a-c), cycle stability (d), dQ/dV peak intensity versus cycle number (e-g), alloying region cyclability (h), and conversion region cyclability (i) between SnS, SnSe and SnTe electrodes.

Rate performance of SnX electrodes. (a-e) Comparison of the discharge-charge curves at different current density (a-c), rate capabilities (d), volumetric capacity (e) between SnS, SnSe and SnTe electrodes. (f) The rate performance of SnTe at various current densities.

Pseudo-capacitive contribution, Li-diffusivity and diffusion energy barrier characterization. (a-c) Typical CV profiles at different scan rates (a), log(i) versus log(v) plots (b), and pseudo-capacitance contribution percentage (c) of SnTe electrode. (d-g) Comparison of the pseudo-capacitance contribution (d), Li-diffusivity (e), EIS profiles (f), and linear fitting of Z′ versus ω 1/2 relationship (g) between SnS, SnSe, and SnTe electrodes. (h,i) The discharge/charge curves (h), schematic illustration and cycling performance (i) of SnTe//AC capacitor.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As same classification of tin-based chalcogenides system, all of the SnX (X=S/Se/Te) should possess similar structural characteristics by our conventional view. However, the experimental SnS&SnSe possess orthorhombic-phase structure while SnTe refers to other cubic-phase. How to explain above structural-transition and quantify the impact on their electrochemical behaviors are remained challenge. Herein, such phenomena are investigated by both experimental-tools and theoretical-calculations. The results show that the integration of S/Se into Sn would trigger serious lattice-distortion, bonding-broken and thus phase-transition from cubic to orthorhombic SnS&SnSe due to their large difference on atomic-radius and electronegativity. While fusing Te atom into Sn to form the SnTe enables a well maintained cubic-structure phase like Sn matrix owing to their similar physicochemical properties. Benefitted from above structure characteristics, the SnTe enables to keep Sn-like metallic conductivity (3.31×103 S m−1), larger tap-density (6.48 g cm−3) and better reaction-kinetic, thus delivering better cyclability (698 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles) and rate performance (236 mAh g−1 at 10A g−1) for LIBs. Besides, the SnTe even exhibits higher pseudo-capacitance contribution (94%) and thereby, the assembled AC//SnTe Li-ion capacitor enables high energy & power density (114 Wh kg−1, 7000 W kg−1) and superior cyclability (92.7%-retention after 8000 cycles) for lithium-storage. The above understanding of structure-transition and electrochemical-evolution may open avenue for material design towards advanced energy storage.

The structural transition from orthorhombic SnS & SnSe to cubic SnTe can be greatly revealed with theoretical and fundamental atomic analysis. Cubic SnTe enables a higher conductivity, larger tap density and better Li-diffusivity, thus delivering better cyclability (698 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles) and rate performance (236 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1) for LIBs. Besides, owing its high pseudo-capacitance contribution, the fabricated AC//SnTe capacitor achieves large energy density (114 Wh kg−1), high power density (7000 W kg−1) and superior cyclability (92.7% retention after 8000 cycles) for advanced lithium storage.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of portable electronics and electric vehicles (EV) market, pushing the higher energy & power density of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) has become highly desired to meet the long driving mileages and fast charging speed demands [1–4]. At such situation, the recent commercial graphite anode has become the limiting component, as its theoretical capacity is only 372 mAh g−1 which is proved not large enough to meet above increasing energy density requirement (>500 Wh kg−1) [5–7]. Besides, it also suffers the sluggish Li+ desolvation at graphite surface and much low lithiated potential (∼0.1 V vs. Li+/Li), which is easy to trigger Li dendrite formation, safety concern and thus possible failure of fast charging ability [8–10]. In comparison, the recent discovered tin-based chalcogenides, e.g., SnS & SnSe, have attracted increasing attention due to their unique layered structure and high reactivity for lithium storage [11,12]. On the one hand, typical SnS & SnSe belong to one kind dual-Li-active metal sulfides, in which both of the anionic S/Se and cationic Sn enable to multi-electronic react with lithium by firstly conversion (Li2S/Li2Se) following alloying (Li x Sn) process, thus contributing large capacity over 1000 mAh g−1, two times higher than that of graphite [13–16]. Besides, the SnS & SnSe possess a unique layered structure with large enough interlayer spacing, which offers the natural channels for quick Li-ion diffusion towards fast charging ability [17–19]. In addition, compared to ordinary metal oxides, such SnS & SnSe account for a higher electronic conductivity as well, thus better reaction kinetics for long cyclability and smaller voltage polarization towards larger energy density can be expected [20–22]. With above great advantages, the recent discovered SnS & SnSe are considered the alternative anode materials for next generation LIBs.

In addition to above SnS & SnSe, another tin-based chalcogenide, e.g., the SnTe as an emerging star material, also shows high potential in the field of lithium storage in recent years [23]. Similar to SnS & SnSe analogues, when served as anode for LIBs, this SnTe enables a large reversible capacity over 700 mAh g−1 as well considering its dual-Li-active property (Li2Te, Li x Sn) for energy storage [24,25]. More specially, typical SnTe possesses comparable yet higher tap-density of 6.5 g cm−3 than SnS (4.5 g cm−3) and SnSe (6.18 g cm−3), which is believed to promote the energy density for LIBs. Besides, the SnTe even exhibits higher metallic conductivity of ∼3.31×103 S m−1 than semiconducting SnS (∼1.00×10−1 S m−1) and SnSe (∼1.25×100 S m−1), thus facilitating better reaction kinetics and electrochemical performances as well. Therefore, all of these SnX (X=S, Se, Te) chalcogenides show high potential for advanced lithium storage. However, difference is that compared to SnS & SnSe whose phase structure belong to an orthorhombic layered system, the SnTe is found possess another different cubic phase structure, which is in contradictory to the conventional view that all of these SnX should possess the same structural characteristics [26–29]. Considering their same classification of tin-based chalcogenides system, it is hard to explain the above structural difference by traditional views. As well known, the electrochemical behaviors of materials are closely related to their intrinsic structural characteristics [30–32]. Since the SnTe shows a different cubic structure phase from orthorhombic SnS & SnSe, it would deliver some different electrochemical behaviors and thus unexpected battery performances for lithium storage [33]. However, the structure transition relationship and structure-oriented electrochemical evolution between SnX have not been fully investigated up to now. How to experimentally explain the above structural transition and quantify the impact on their electrochemical behaviors resulted from structural evolution are of great interest for material design and energy storage.

In this work, series of SnX chalcogenides are synthesized by a high-energy ball-milled process. As proved, the SnS & SnSe are found to possess an orthorhombic phase structure while SnTe refers to cubic phase. Such structural transition is fully investigated by both experimental tools and theoretical calculations. The results show that the integration of anionic S/Se into cationic Sn matrix would trigger serious lattice distortion, bonding broken and thus phase transition from cubic to orthorhombic system due to their large difference on atomic radius and electronegativity between S/Se and Sn. While fusing Te atom into Sn to form the SnTe enables a well maintained cubic-structure phase like Sn matrix owing to their similar physicochemical properties. Benefitted from above structure characteristics, the SnTe with cubic phase structure enables a higher conductivity (3.31×103 S m−1), larger tap density (6.48 g cm−3) and better reaction kinetic than SnS & SnSe, thus delivering better cyclability (>200 cycles) and rate performance (236 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1) for LIBs. Besides, the SnTe even enables a higher pseudo-capacitance contribution (94% at 1 mV s−1) and thereby, offering the possibility to assemble with active carbon (AC), and the formed AC//SnTe Li-ion capacitor enables a large energy density (114 Wh kg−1), high power density (7000 W kg−1) and superior cyclability (92.7% retention after 8000 cycles) for lithium storage. The above understanding of structure transition and electrochemical evolution may open an avenue for material design towards advanced lithium storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of SnX (X=S, Se, Te)
ce:section.ce:para:
Series of SnX chalcogenides were synthesized by a high-energy ball-milled method. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of synthesized products were performed in Fig. 1 a–c and Fig. S1. It can be seen that the SnS & SnSe delivered one kind similar XRD pattern while that of SnTe exhibited another different one, revealing the similar structures between SnS and SnSe while another phase structure of SnTe. To gain further insight into crystal structure and cell parameters of above samples, the XRD Rietveld refinements were performed using GSAS software package. All of the error factor (R wp) were controlled within 0.06, revealing their high Rietveld quality. The Rietveld phase structure, cell parameters and atomic coordinates after refinement were shown in Fig. 1d, Fig. S2 and Table S1. Just as expected, both of the SnS and SnSe can be well indexed by a layered orthorhombic system in space group 62, Pnma. Difference is that the unit cell volume increased from 193.189 Å3 (SnS) to 210.481 Å3 (SnSe), which may be ascribed to the larger atomic radius of Se (103 p.m.) than S (88 p.m.) triggering its volume expansion. While for the synthesized SnTe, it can be well referred to a novel cubic system in space group 225, Fm-3m, which is totally different from SnS and SnSe analogues. The Raman scattering as a sensitive local probe also detected the above structural evolution as shown in Fig. 1e. The SnS delivered a black phosphorus-like Raman vibration modes, e.g.,
A g
(92.7 cm−1),
B
3 g
(160.2 cm −1),
A g
(185.2 cm−1),
A g
(220.0 cm−1), suggesting its typical black P-like layered structure characteristic [26]. Similarly, the Raman peaks of SnSe were found to be highly similar to that of SnS, e.g.
A g
(67.6 cm−1),
B
3 g
(98.5 cm−1),
A g
(148.6 cm−1),
A g
(181.4 cm−1), revealing their same layered orthorhombic structure [34]. Considering the larger relative molecular mass of SnSe than SnS, the characteristic Raman peaks of SnSe were shifted to lower wavenumbers. However, for the synthesized SnTe product, it exhibited completely different vibration modes from SnS & SnSe, e.g.
A 1
: 119.7 cm−1 and
E TO
: 139.0 cm−1, which can be well referred to a cubic atomic arrangement [27]. To explore the local microstructure in detail, the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were further performed in Fig. 1f–h and Fig. S3. It can be seen that all of these SnX exhibited typical ring-like diffraction patterns, suggesting their intrinsoc polycrystalline property. Specifically, the SnS delivered several diffraction rings in Fig. 1f1, which can be well indexed to its (201), (111), (311), and (501) crystal planes, respectively. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1f2-3 also detected the d-spacing of 2.727 Å, corresponding to its (111) plane. Similarly, the SnSe in Fig. 1g1-3 exhibited comparable diffraction rings yet larger (111) interplanar d-spacing (2.891 Å) than SnS (2.727 Å), which may be ascribed to its larger atomic Se radius (103 p.m.) than S (88 p.m.) triggering its interlayer expansion. While for the SnTe sample, it exhibited different diffraction spots and rings from SnS & SnSe. In Fig. 1h1, two characteristic diffraction rings associated to cubic phase can be well observed, corresponding to the (200) and (220) planes of SnTe. The HRTEM in Fig. 1h2 also detected its interplanar d-spacing of 3.136 Å, corresponding to its typical (200) plane as well. Besides, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were performed in Fig. S4, in which all of the SnX samples showed comparable and similar morphology, with particle size of 8∼10 μm. Further elemental mapping images revealed that the elemental Sn and X had been uniformly distribution with each other at atomic level, suggesting their full reaction after continuous ball milling process. Consequently, series of SnX were successfully synthesized, in which the SnS & SnSe were interestingly found to possess orthorhombic layered structure while SnTe refers to other cubic phase at normal temperatures and pressures (NTP).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation to reveal the structural evolution between SnX
ce:section.ce:para:
As the same classification of tin-based chalcogenides system, it is considered that these SnX (X=S, Se, Te) should possess a same structure characteristic and similar physicochemical properties by our conventional view. However, the synthesized SnS & SnSe at NTP are referred to orthorhombic cell in Pnma space group, while that SnTe product is indexed to the other cubic system in Fm-3m space group. Such different phase structures and unexpected structural evolution is in contradictory to our traditional views. According to above XRD Rietveld refinement, the SnTe possess the cubic structure, different from its hexagonal Te substance but similar to its tetragonal Sn matrix structure phase. We therefore assumed that these SnX may be achieved by introducing S/Se/Te atoms into Sn matrix and substituting partial Sn atoms at defined ratios. Based on this hypothesis, we proposed the possible structural evolution of SnX as shown in Fig. 2 a. All of the SnS, SnSe and SnTe were achieved by replacing partial Sn atoms in tetragonal Sn structure with S, Se and Te atoms, respectively. While for the SnS model, there is a large difference on their atomic radius and electronegativity between cationic Sn (χ Sn=1.96, R Sn=145 p.m.) and anionic S (χ S=2.58, R S=88 p.m.). Such difference (ΔR=65%, Δχ=0.62) is large enough to trigger serious electron drift, lattice distortions and thus bonding broken, which would finally change the original tetragonal Sn structure matrix and facilitate to the new orthorhombic phase formation for SnS product. Similarly, for the SnSe sample, there is also large difference on their atomic radius (ΔR=41%) and electronegativity (Δχ=0.59) between Sn and Se (χ Se=2.55, R Se=103 p.m.) and thereby, resulting in lattice distortions and phase transition from tetragonal to orthorhombic cell. In comparison, the introduction of Te into SnTe model, which exhibits comparable atomic radius (R Te=123 p.m., R Sn=145 p.m., ΔR=15%) and similar electronegativity (χ Te=2.10, χ Sn=1.96, Δχ=0.14) with Sn atoms, promises Te to effectively substitute into Sn matrix without obvious lattice distortion and structural damage. Consequently, cubic phase SnTe formation through one kind substitutional solid solution process can be achieved, keeping the original tetragonal Sn-like structure without change. As proved, the calculated formation energy (E for) of series SnX was further performed to demonstrate their structural stability using following equation.
(1)
E for
=
E SnX
−
μ Sn
−
μ X
In this equation, the E SnX represents the total ground state energy of SnX, the μ Sn denotes the chemical potential of Sn atom (space group I41/amd), and μ X is the chemical potential of X atom in S (space group Fddd), Se (space group P3121), or Te (space group P312). Accordingly, the E for of SnS, SnSe and SnTe were calculated to be −0.974, −0.955 eV and −0.653 eV respectively, whose negative values demonstrated their thermodynamic stabilization at normal temperatures and pressures. Besides, the theoretical phonon spectra of series SnX were also performed in Fig. 2b. It can be seen that there is no imaginary frequency mode of the phonon dispersion relations for orthorhombic SnS & SnSe and cubic SnTe, demonstrating their structural stability as well. For a better understanding, the orthorhombic phase SnTe model was constructed for comparison, whose E for was calculated to a positive value (0.113 eV). Besides, there was abundant imaginary frequency mode of its phonon dispersion relations for the orthorhombic Pnma-SnTe, suggesting its structural instability as orthorhombic phase state. Consequently, combining with theoretical calculation and fundamental atomic analysis, the structural phase transition from orthorhombic SnS & SnSe to cubic SnTe can be greatly revealed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of electronic structure evolution and bonding interaction between SnX
ce:section.ce:para: Since the cubic SnTe exhibits different phase structure from orthorhombic SnS & SnSe, there may be some differences on their electronic structure, bonding interactions and electrical conductivity. Therefore, the plots of electron location function were calculated and performed in Fig. 3 a by first principles calculations. It can be seen that obvious electron cloud polarization occurred in both SnS and SnSe, which suggests the presence of robust Sn-S or Sn-Se bonding interactions. In contrast, the electrons in nearby Sn and Te atoms in SnTe exhibited little polarization, suggesting its relatively weakened Sn-Te interaction. As proved, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of series SnX were further performed in Fig. 3b. Similarly, when changed SnS & SnSe to SnTe, the shift of Sn 3d3/2 (from 495.68 eV to 494.18 eV) and Sn 3d5/2 (from 487.33 eV to 485.78 eV) to lower binding energy location can be observed, suggesting a weaker Sn-Te bonding interaction of SnTe. Compared to SnS which possessed 91% high-valence Sn state (color pink area) yet only 9% low-valence Sn state (color purple area), the SnTe sample even delivered total 100% low-valence Sn (color purple part) without high-valence Sn state. Such phenomenon may be ascribed the larger radius and smaller electronegativity of anionic Te atom (R Te=123 p.m., χ Te=2.10) than S (R S=88 p.m., χ S=2.58) and Se (R Se=103 p.m., χ Se=2.55), weakening the electron-transporting ability and Sn-Te bonding interaction. Besides, the high-resolution S2p spectrum of SnS can be decomposed into S 2p1/2 at 162.28 eV and S 2p3/2 at 161.08 eV respectively, corresponding to its full oxidation state of S2− [35]. Similarly, the high-resolution Se 3d spectrum of SnSe can be also decomposed into Se 3d3/2 at 54.96 eV and Se 3d5/2 at 54.16 eV respectively, corresponding to its close oxidation state of Se2− [36]. For the SnTe, however, its deconvoluted Te 3d3/2 and Te 3d5/2 were located at 587.28, 583.48, 576.88, and 573.08 eV respectively, corresponding to a close but not full oxidation state of Te2− [37]. Such electron position function and XPS results are in accordance with the analysis of previous formation energy calculations, in which the smaller E for value of SnTe suggested its weakened Sn-Te interaction as well. Besides, the calculated density of states (DOS) was performed in Fig. 3c. It can be seen that typical SnS and SnSe delivered band gap of 0.654 and 0.327 eV respectively, suggesting their intrinsic semiconductor properties. While the SnTe exhibited an attractive metallic conductivity, since there is no band gap on its fermi energy. Such higher electronic conductivity of SnTe than SnS & SnSe may be attributed to its well remaining metallic Sn-like structure phase. Proved by the experimental powder conductivity measurement in Fig. 3d, the conductivity value of SnTe is detected high up to ∼3.31×103 S m−1, 3-4 orders of magnitude higher than SnS (∼1.00×10−1 S m−1) and SnSe (∼1.25×100 S m−1), revealing its metallic conductivity property. Besides, the thermal-gravimetric (TG) curves were performed in Fig. 3e, in which the SnTe showed a comparable yet little smaller decomposition temperatures of 548 °C than SnS (611 °C) and SnSe (568 °C), which may be ascribed to its smaller formation energy and weakened Sn-Te bonding interaction. Nevertheless, such high decomposition temperatures are large enough to guarantee the structural stability and safety for battery operation. Furthermore, the powder compaction density of series SnX were performed in Fig. 3f. Compared to recent commercial graphite anode whose tap density was only 2.25 g cm−3, the SnS, SnSe and SnTe showed much higher tap density of 5.22 g cm−3, 6.18 g cm−3 and 6.48 g cm−3 respectively, almost three times higher than that of graphite. Among them, the SnTe product exhibited the highest compaction density owing to its cubic atomic arrangement structure, which is considered to promote the volumetric energy density for LIBs.

Cycling stability characterization: Since the orthorhombic SnS & SnSe show unique layered structure for quick ion diffusion and cubic SnTe exhibits enhanced conductivity for better reaction kinetics, the impact on their electrochemical behavior resulted from structural evolution was further investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 4 a–c, when served as anode for LIBs, all of these SnS, SnSe and SnTe electrodes delivered large discharge/charge capacity of 1183/1259, 889/945 and 728/801 mAh g−1, with high initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) high up to 94%, 94% and 91% respectively, revealing their high reversibility for lithium storage. Further differential capacity plots (DCPs, dQ/dV curves) were performed in Fig. S5, in which all of these SnX electrodes exhibited two pairs of reduction and oxidation peaks, corresponding to their conversion reaction (e.g. SnX+2Li+→Li2X + Sn, 0.9-2.0 V) and subsequent alloying reaction (Sn+xLi→Li x Sn, 0.005-0.9 V), respectively [13,38,39]. Such two-step Li-storage mechanism was proved by ex-situ XRD measurement in Fig. S6 as well, in which both of the lithiated Li2X and Li x Sn products can be well detected. When conducted at a high current density of 500 mA g−1 in Fig. 4d, the SnS & SnSe electrodes suffered fast capacity fading, with only remaining 208 mAh g−1 (17% retention) and 461 mAh g−1 (61% retention) after 200 cycles. In comparison, the SnTe electrode enabled a large reversible capacity of 698 mAh g−1 and almost 95% retention after same 200 cycles, with coulombic efficiency consistently close to 100% at each cycle, suggesting its better cyclability. To investigate above cycling differences between SnX, their in-situ dQ/dV profiles during previous 100 cycles were performed in Fig. 4e–g and Fig. S7. It can be seen that all of these SnX electrodes enabled to well keep their dQ/dV redox peaks at ∼0.6 V associated to Li x Sn alloying region without attenuation. While the dQ/dV peaks at ∼1.5 V related to conversion reaction region showed large differences. Specifically, the SnS & SnSe electrodes suffered fast attenuation even disappear of their dQ/dV peak at ∼1.5 V (Li2S and Li2Se) after 100 cycles. However, the SnTe electrode enabled a well maintaining of its reversible dQ/dV peak of Li2Te. That is, it is the conversion reaction kinetic difference between Li2S, Li2Se and Li2Te that resulted in their cyclability distinction. The lithiated Li2S & Li2Se products in SnS & SnSe electrodes may undergo more serious polysulfide shuttling difficulty, solubility problems and low conductivity than Li2Te, thus resulting sluggish reaction kinetics and thus fast capacity fading [40]. In comparison, the Li2Te in SnTe showed much higher reversibility and enhanced reaction kinetics. In Fig. S6, the characteristic XRD signals of Li2Te greatly emerged when discharged to 0.7 V and repeatedly disappeared after charged back to 3 V, revealing its full decomposition for advanced lithium storage. The ex-situ SEM observations on electrode cross-sections in Fig. S8 also provided further insight into the electrode stability. The SnTe electrode exhibited a thickness increasement from 16.47 to 20.65 μm after full lithiation, combining with a volume expansion of only 25%, which was lower than that of SnS (39%, from 17.55 to 24.47 μm) and SnSe (29%, from 18.46 to 23.87 μm). Moreover, after full de-lithiation, the electrode thickness of SnTe was recovered to 17.12 μm again, corresponding to an expansivity of 4% compared to its fresh electrode state, much lower than SnS and SnSe (both ∼5%) as well. Therefore, the SnTe enabled an electrode-reversibility and morphology integrity, thus promoting its cycle stability to a certain degree. Besides, the cubic phase characteristic of SnTe promised itself with higher metallic conductivity than SnS & SnSe, which is considered to facilitate its cycling optimization as well. As proved in Fig. 4h–i, when cycled at alloying region (0.005-0.9 V) and conversion (0.9-3.0 V) range respectively, all of the SnX electrodes enabled a stable alloying-based cyclability (>90% retention after 500 cycles) yet unsatisfying conversion-based circulation (<65% retention after 350 cycles). Nevertheless, the SnTe enabled a better conversion-based cycle performance, with higher capacity retention of 62% than SnS (45.8%) and SnSe (47.5%). Therefore, benefitted from the metallic conductivity of cubic phase and higher reversibility of Te component, the SnTe electrode enables a better reaction kinetic and cyclability than SnS & SnSe analogues.

Rate performance characterization: In addition to above cyclability, the rate performance of series SnX electrodes associated to their fast charge/discharge ability were also performed in Fig. 5 a–c and Table S2. Obviously, the SnS electrode suffered fast capacity fading from 1322 mAh g−1 to 193 mAh g−1 (14.6%) along with increased current density from 0.1C to 5C, combining with enlarged voltage polarization between discharge/charge plateaus even disappear of its charge platform at high current density. In comparison, the SnTe electrode enabled a better rate performance than SnS & SnSe, with large reversible capacity of 518 mAh g−1 (51.0% retention) and stable discharge-charge plateaus at same high current density of 5C. In Fig. 5d, when charged back to 0.1C again, the SnTe electrode still enabled a capacity recovery of almost 100% as well, much higher than that of SnS (∼85%) and SnSe (∼92%). Such better rate capability of SnTe may be attributed to its cubic Sn-like structure, endowing itself with higher metallic conductivity towards better reaction kinetic. Though quite a little low on its capacity, the SnTe still shows great advantage when considering its large tap density (6.48 g cm−3) for high volumetric capacity. As shown in Fig. 5e, the SnTe electrode delivered comparable large volumetric capacity of 6581 mAh cm−3 similar to that of SnS & SnSe, making a balance between specific capacity and energy density. Furthermore, such advantage of high volumetric capacity even becomes obvious when operated at high current density. When operated at 5C, the SnTe electrode still enabled a large volumetric capacity of 3292 mAh cm−3, much higher than that of SnS (1337 mAh cm−3) and SnSe (3145 mAh cm−3), suggesting its great advantage at high current density operation. Consequently, when further operated at higher capacity of 10 A g−1 (14.4C) in Fig. 5f, a large reversible capacity of 236 mAh g−1 can still be obtained for the SnTe electrode. More impressively, it even demonstrated a 100% capacity recovery when gradually returned current density back to initial 100 mA g−1 again, suggesting its high reversibility of SnTe electrode. When cycled at high current density of 5 A g−1 in Fig. S9, it still achieved a substantial capacity of 469 mAh g−1 with a high retention of 75% after 600 cycles. Such superior electrochemical performances of SnTe has surpassed recent reported chalcogenides as shown in Table S3 and Fig. S10. Therefore, benefitted from the cubic Sn-like structure towards high metallic conductivity, the SnTe product enabled a good reaction kinetic and superior rate capability for fast charging LIBs.

Pseudo-capacitive contribute characterization: To further investigate the Li-storage mechanism associated to reaction kinetics evolution between series SnX, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements at different scan rates were performed in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S11a–b. It can be seen that the detected current was gradually enhanced along with its increased sweep rate for all these SnX electrodes. The relationship between current (i) and sweep rate (v) can be obtained by the Randles-Sevcik equation:
(2)
i = a
v b
e . g . , log
i = b log v + log a

According to above equation, when the b value is defined to 0.5, the Li storage process is predominantly controlled by diffusion behavior. While the b value close to 1.0 refers a pseudo-capacitive contribution [41]. Accordingly, the linear log(i) versus log(v) relationships are shown in Fig. 6b and Fig. S11c–d. During alloying region, the b 1 /b 2 values of SnS, SnSe, and SnTe electrodes were calculated to be 0.72/0.84, 0.71/0.82, and 0.75/0.92 respectively, suggesting their combination of both diffusion and pseudo-capacitance behavior for kinetic. Similarly, during conversion region, the b 3 /b 4 values were calculated to be 0.52/0.48 and 0.62/0.49 for SnX electrodes as well. Accordingly, the larger b 1 /b 2 values than b 3 /b 4 revealed their higher pseudo-capacitance contribution in alloying region than conversion step. Among them, the SnTe electrode exhibits the highest b-value of up to 0.92, revealing its largest pseudo-capacitance contribution for lithium storage. The specific capacitance and diffusion contribution at a given scan rate can be further quantified by the following equations [42,43].

As illustrated in Fig. 6c–d and Fig. S11e–f, when increased sweep rate from 0.2 to 1.0 mV s−1, the SnTe electrode enabled a pseudo-capacitive contribution from 73% to 94%, much higher than SnS (50%-83%) and SnSe (71%-92%). Considering its low surface area of only 29.9 m2 g−1 and few porous distribution (N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm in Fig. S12), such large pseudo-capacitive contribution of SnTe electrode may be attributed to its intrinsic multi-valence state property and high elemental reactivity, rather than its surface areas. Besides, the cubic phase structure oriented metallic conductivity is considered to promote the reaction kinetics and reversibility for lithium storage as well.

Li-diffusivity characterization: To quantify the specific Li-diffusivity (D Li+) of these SnX electrodes, the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) was conducted and performed in Fig. S13. According to GITT curves, the D Li + can be calculated by following simplified Fick's Second Law equation

In this equation, I 0 denotes the electric current, S is the interfacial area, Z
i is defined the charge transfer number, and dE/dt
1/2 refers the differential with potential to square root of time for each single-step. Accordingly, the values of D Li+ of SnX electrodes at various discharge/charge states were performed in Fig. 6e. It is noteworthy that SnTe electrode, whether in lithiation or de-lithiation process, always showed a comparable yet little higher D Li + than SnS & SnSe, suggesting its faster diffusion kinetic towards better rate capability. Similarly, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were also conducted in Fig. 6f, in which the SnTe electrode delivered a much lower charge transfer resistance (R ct) of 91.0 Ω compared to that of SnS (205.7 Ω) and SnSe (151.8 Ω), revealing its faster charge transportation. Furthermore, according to the low-frequency line of EIS curves in Fig. 6g and Fig. S14 and Equations S1-2, the D Li+ of SnTe electrode can be calculated to be 1.24×10−10 cm2 s−1, much higher than SnS (5.52×10−11 cm2 s−1) and SnSe (7.57×10−11 cm2 s−1) as well, consistent with above GITT measurements. Besides, the specific Li-adsorption energy, Li-diffusion paths and associated energy barrier in SnX were simulated and performed in Fig. S15–16. All of the SnX materials exhibited the negative adsorption energy values, e.g. −0.47 eV of SnS, −0.38 eV of SnSe and −0.25 eV of SnTe, suggesting their preference of Li-adsorption and transportation. However, the cubic SnTe enabled a homogeneous and little smaller Li-diffusion energy barrier (0.474–0.522 eV) than anisotropic SnS (0.139–0.686 eV) and SnSe (0.186–0.544 eV) owing to its isotropic property. Consequently, the orthorhombic to cubic phase transition endows SnTe with a higher pseudo-capacitive contribution, better Li-diffusivity and reaction kinetics for advanced lithium storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium-ion capacitor assembled with active carbon cathode and SnTe anode
ce:section.ce:para: According to above investigation of electrochemical mechanism behavior, the SnTe sample showed a high pseudo-capacitive contribution up to 94% for lithium storage, which offers the possibility to assemble with active carbon (AC) to fabricate an advanced lithium-ion capacitor (LIC) for high power energy storage. Specific schematic was illustrated in Fig. 6i, in which the LIC was assembled with SnTe as anode and active carbon (AC) as cathode using 1 M LiPF6 dissolved in EC/DMC/EMC electrolyte. During the charged process, the Li+ are lithiated into SnTe anode, while the PF6 − from electrolyte are adsorbed on the surface of AC cathode. Following discharge process is reversed the charge procedure. The typical discharge/charge curves ranging from 0.1 to 5 A g−1 were performed in Fig. 6h, which always showed an attractive symmetrical triangle shape in any current density, revealing its good reversibility and fast kinetics of this LIC [44,45]. Furthermore, the CV measurements at different sweep rates were shown in Fig. S17. Even conducted at 20 mV s−1, this SnTe based LIC still exhibited a rectangular-like pattern, achieving a coexistence of both Faraday and non-Faraday reaction [46]. Fig. S18 illustrated the impressive energy and power density of such assembled LIC, which enabled an energy density up to 114 Wh kg−1 (Equations S3–5, based on the total mass of two electrode active material) at a high-power density of 1400 W kg−1. Even at a high-power density of 7000 W kg−1, this SnTe based LIC still provided 31 Wh kg−1 for energy output, demonstrating its robust competitiveness in energy and power density when compared to other reported chalcogenides based capacitors [47–55]. Besides, the long cycle stability of such LIC was shown in Fig. 6i, in which the LIC enabled a capacity retention of 92.7% after 8000 cycles at 5 A g−1, with only 0.0009% drop per cycle, revealing its excellent durability and stability for advanced energy storage.

In summary, series SnX (X=S, Se, Te) are synthesized by high-energy ball-milled method in this work. It is interestingly found the SnS & SnSe possess an orthorhombic phase structure while SnTe refers to the other cubic phase, which is in contradictory to our conventional view that all of these SnX should possess the same structural characteristics considering their same classification to tin-based chalcogenides. Various experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations are performed to investigate such structural evolution. The results show that due to the large difference on atomic radius and electronegativity between S/Se and Sn, the integration of anionic S/Se into cationic Sn matrix would trigger serious lattice distortion, bonding broken and thus phase transition from cubic to orthorhombic SnS & SnSe. While fusing Te atom into Sn to form the SnTe enables a well maintained cubic-structure phase like Sn matrix owing to their similar physicochemical properties. Benefitted from the remaining cubic phase structure, the SnTe product enables to keep the Sn-like isotropic property, higher conductivity (3.31×103 S m−1), larger tap density (6.48 g cm−3) and better Li-diffusivity (1.24×10−10 cm2 s−1) than SnS & SnSe, thus delivering better cyclability (698 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles) and rate performance (236 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1) for LIBs. Besides, considering its high pseudo-capacitance contribution (94%), the SnTe enables to assemble with active carbon to fabricate AC//SnTe Li-ion capacitor, which achieves large energy density (114 Wh kg−1), high power density (7000 W kg−1) and superior cyclability (92.7% retention after 8000 cycles) for fast lithium storage. The above understanding of structure transition relationship and structure oriented electrochemical behavior evolution may open an avenue for material design towards advanced energy storage.

Material synthesis: Series of SnX (X=S, Se, Te) were synthesized via a typical high-energy mechanical ball milling method. Specifically, the pure Sn (99.9%, Macklin) and S (99.9%, Macklin), Se (99.9%, Macklin) or Te (99.9%, Macklin) powders in defined molar ratio of 1:1 were placed into a stainless steel vessel under Ar atmosphere. The SnX products can be achieved after ball-milling with a rotational speed of 400 rpm for 18 h.

Material characterization: The synthesized samples were characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D2 PHASER), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Supra), Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific DXRxi with a 532 nm excitation), powder resistivity tester (ST2722-SZ) and thermogravimetric analysis (TG, TA-Q600). The morphology and microstructure of products were measured by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Phenom ProX, 10 kV), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL The Standard of Japanese Electrical Manufacture's Association (JEM) 2100). The XRD Rietveld refinements were performed using GSAS software package.

Electrochemical characterization: The SnX active materials were first manual mortar-and-pestle mixed with conductive carbon (Super P) and lithiated polyacrylic acid (Li-PAA) in a mass ratio of 7:2:1. For the long cycle and rate performance testing, the slurry was prepared via mechanical ball milling above substances. Subsequently, the formed slurry was poured onto a Cu foil following a drying process of 80 °C for 12 h under vacuum. The CR2025 coin type half-cell was assembled in a glove box under Ar atmosphere (H2O, O2<1 ppm) using SnX as working electrode and lithium metal as counter electrode. The experimental electrolyte was the 1.0 mol L−1 LiPF6 dissolved in ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) (EC:DMC:EMC=1:1:1 vol%) solution. The discharge/charge profiles, dQ/dV curves, and cycling stability at galvanostatic or different current rates were performed on the Neware battery test system (CT-4008T-5 V 10 mA-164, 25 °C) and Hokuto Denko automatic battery testing system (HJ1001SD8, Gifu, Japan). The 1C-rates of SnS, SnSe and SnTe were defined based on their specific theoretical capacity, e.g. 1138 mA g−1, 868 mA g−1 and 696 mA g−1, respectively. The EIS measurement was performed on a high precision electrochemical impedance testing system (Bio-Logic, VSP-300, France). The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of lithium ion capacitors are calculated by Equations S(3)–(5).

Calculations details: All of the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [56]. The electron-ion interactions were represented by projector-augmented wave (PAW) pseudopotentials, and the electron exchange-correlation interactions were treated by Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional with generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [57,58]. The cutoff energy was set to 520 eV for calculation entirety, and the convergence criteria were 1×10−6 eV for energy and 0.02 eV Å−1 for force. To ensure consistency in the calculations, the Brillouin zones were sampled using the Monkhorst-Pack 3×8×7, 3×8×7 and 5×5×5 k-point grids for geometric optimization of bulk SnS, SnSe and SnTe, respectively [59]. The Li+ migration paths and associated energy barriers were obtained through the nudged elastic band (NEB) method.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xuhao Liu received his Master of Engineering degree from Hainan University in 2024. He is currently a Ph.D. candidate in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Huazhong University of Science and Technology (HUST). His research focuses on anode materials and battery recycling.

Yaqing Wei earned his Doctor of Engineering degree from Huazhong University of Science and Technology (HUST) in 2020 and is currently an Associate Professor at the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hainan University. His research focuses on large-capacity anode materials and novel high-entropy materials.

Huiqiao Li received her B.S. degree in chemistry from Zhengzhou University in 2003, and then received her Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry from Fudan University in 2008. Afterwards, she worked as a postdoctoral fellow for 4 years at the Energy Technology Research Institute, National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Japan. Currently, she is a full Professor of School of Materials Science and Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology (HUST). Her research interests include energy storage materials and electrochemical power sources such as Lithium/Sodium-ion batteries, Flexible energy storage devices, Solid-state batteries, and In-situ micro/nano characterization.
